ja.suggestion: Avoid direct mention of death in Japanese professional contexts — use indirect phrasing or euphemisms.
4 (shi) sounds like death in Japanese — avoid in gift quantities or formal contexts.
9 (ku) sounds like suffering in Japanese — avoid in gift quantities.
Foxes (kitsune) have supernatural connotations in Japanese folklore — use carefully in metaphors.
Direct commands are considered rude in Japanese — use polite request forms (～てください or ～ていただけますか).
Direct refusal is uncommon in Japanese — indirect expressions like 'it may be difficult' are preferred.
Tipping is considered rude in Japan — it implies the service was not good enough without it.
Funeral references are extremely sensitive — avoid casual use and use appropriate honorific language.
4 (si) sounds like death in Chinese — avoid in gifts, sets, floor numbers.
9 (ku) sounds like suffering in Japanese — avoid in gift quantities.

ar.suggestion: The left hand is considered unclean in Arab culture — specify right hand for giving/receiving objects.
Pork is forbidden in Islam — substitute with halal alternatives in food-related contexts.
Alcohol is forbidden in Islam — avoid in formal or professional contexts with Arab audiences.
Dogs are considered unclean in Islamic tradition — avoid using dog metaphors or casual references.
Inshallah is a sincere religious expression, not just a vague response — treat with appropriate respect.
Pointing with the index finger is rude in Arab cultures — use an open hand gesture instead.
During Ramadan, eating/drinking in public is disrespectful — acknowledge the holy month appropriately.
References to Israel are politically sensitive in many Arab-speaking regions — use neutral phrasing.

hi.suggestion: Cows are sacred in Hinduism — avoid negative references or food-related contexts.
Beef consumption is taboo in Hindu culture — substitute with neutral food references.
The left hand is considered inauspicious — use right hand references for giving/receiving.
Leather products (especially cowhide) are offensive in Hindu contexts — specify material type carefully.
Widows face significant stigma in traditional Indian contexts — use sensitive, neutral language.
Caste references are extremely sensitive in India — avoid unless specifically necessary.
Namaste is a respectful greeting — do not trivialise or use as a joke.
Use indirect terms like 'washroom' or 'facilities' in formal Hindi contexts.
Beef/steak consumption is taboo in Hindu culture — substitute with neutral food references.

zh.suggestion: 4 (si) sounds like death in Chinese — avoid in gifts, phone numbers, floor numbers.
Gifting a clock sounds like attending a funeral in Chinese culture — avoid as gift reference.
A green hat (戴绿帽子) signifies infidelity in Chinese culture — never gift or reference.
Sharing a pear (分梨) sounds like separation — avoid in relationship contexts.
Umbrella (伞) sounds like scatter/disperse — avoid as a gift.
Taiwan is politically extremely sensitive in mainland China — use neutral phrasing or avoid.
Tiananmen Square events are heavily censored — avoid in Chinese-language content.
Face (面子, mianzi) is a critical social concept — never publicly embarrass someone or cause loss of face.
4 (si) sounds like death in Chinese — avoid in gifts, sets, floor numbers.
9 (ku) sounds like suffering in Japanese — avoid in gift quantities.

ko.suggestion: 4 (sa) sounds like death in Korean — avoid in gift quantities and formal contexts.
Writing someone's name in red ink symbolises death in Korean culture — avoid.
Fan death is a widely held belief in Korea — avoid dismissing it casually.
Age determines social hierarchy in Korea — always use appropriate honorific speech levels based on age.
Korean culture values speed and efficiency (빨리빨리) — delays may be perceived negatively.
Jeong (정) is a deep emotional bond unique to Korean culture — has no direct English equivalent.
Japanese colonial history is extremely sensitive in Korea — avoid casual references to Japanese-Korean relations.
This is an extremely sensitive historical topic — only reference in appropriate educational contexts.

de.suggestion: Nazi references are extremely sensitive in Germany — avoid casual use.
References to Hitler are legally restricted in Germany — use only in clearly historical educational contexts.
The swastika is illegal in Germany — never use in any context.
Holocaust references require extreme sensitivity and proper historical framing.
East/West Germany division is still a sensitive topic for many Germans — use carefully.
Germans place high value on punctuality — lateness is considered disrespectful.
Germans have strong privacy values (Datenschutz) — avoid personal questions in professional settings.
Professional titles (Dr., Prof.) are important in German culture — always use them in formal address.

fr.suggestion: The tu/vous distinction is critical in French — using tu with a stranger is considered rude. Always prefer vous in professional contexts.
La bise (cheek kiss greeting) has very specific regional rules in France — explain rather than translate literally.
French secularism (laïcité) is a sensitive political topic — treat with care in religious discussions.
France strictly separates church and state — avoid religious expressions in formal/official contexts.
Lunch is a serious, extended meal in French culture — avoid scheduling meetings during this time.
Strikes (grèves) are common and socially accepted in France — do not express negativity about workers' rights.
Corsican independence is a sensitive political issue — use neutral phrasing.
French-Algerian colonial history is extremely sensitive — use careful, neutral language.

es.suggestion: Neutral in Spain ('to grab') but a strong sexual obscenity in Latin America — use 'tomar' or 'agarrar' instead.
Means 'shell' in Spain but a strong sexual obscenity in Argentina/Chile — use 'caracola' instead.
Means 'bug' in Spain but can be offensive in some Latin American countries — use 'insecto' instead.
Machismo is a complex cultural concept — avoid using 'macho' casually as it has deep cultural implications.
Siesta is a cultural practice but should not be used as a stereotype — use respectfully.
Spanish varies significantly between Spain and Latin America — specify regional variant when accuracy matters.
Vosotros is only used in Spain — Latin American Spanish uses ustedes for all plural address.
Indigenous peoples and their rights are a sensitive political topic in Latin America — use respectful, accurate terminology.

pt.suggestion: Means 'girl' in European Portuguese but 'prostitute' in Brazilian Portuguese — use 'moça' or 'garota' in Brazilian contexts.
Means 'queue' in Portugal but is a homophobic slur in Brazil — avoid entirely, use 'fila' instead.
Vulgar in European Portuguese but more casual in Brazilian Portuguese — use context-appropriate alternatives.
Means 'sweater' in Portugal but 'nightgown' in Brazil — specify when clarity is needed.
Means 'cool/great' in Portugal but is not used in Brazil — use 'legal' or 'bacana' for Brazilian audiences.
Amazon deforestation is an extremely sensitive political topic in Brazil — use careful, neutral language.
Carnival has deep cultural significance in Brazil — treat with respect, not as mere entertainment.
Political corruption is a highly sensitive topic in Brazil — use neutral, factual language.

sw.suggestion: Much of Swahili-speaking East Africa is Muslim — pork is forbidden. Use 'nyama' (meat) generically.
Alcohol is discouraged in many Swahili-speaking Muslim communities — use neutral phrasing.
The left hand is considered unclean in East African Muslim communities — specify right hand for giving/receiving.
Elders command deep respect in East African culture — always use respectful address forms.
'Tribe' is considered offensive in many African contexts — use 'ethnic group' or 'community' instead.
Avoid treating Africa as a monolithic entity — Swahili-speaking East Africa has its own distinct cultures.
Ujamaa (African socialism) has specific political meaning in Tanzania — use accurately.
Witchcraft beliefs are serious in many East African communities — never mock or trivialise.
